question: In the context of distributed mutual exclusion, what is the main purpose of a coordinator process? Briefly describe one advantage and one disadvantage of using a centralized coordinator approach.
expectedPoints: The main purpose is to act as a single, central authority that grants permission to processes wishing to enter their critical section.
Advantage: It is simple to implement and ensures fairness and starvation-freedom easily.
Disadvantage: It creates a single point of failure; if the coordinator crashes, the entire system for mutual exclusion is halted.

question: Describe the two phases of the Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol. Explain what happens if the coordinator fails during each phase and how the protocol handles these failures.
expectedPoints: Phase 1 (Voting): The coordinator sends a VOTE_REQUEST to all cohorts. Cohorts respond with a YES vote (if prepared to commit) or a NO vote (if unable to commit).
Phase 2 (Decision): If all votes are YES, the coordinator sends a GLOBAL_COMMIT. If any vote is NO, it sends a GLOBAL_ABORT. Cohorts then acknowledge the decision.
Coordinator fails in Phase 1: Cohorts wait. A new coordinator can be elected, but it cannot know the outcome, potentially leaving resources locked. This leads to blocking.
Coordinator fails in Phase 2: Cohorts that did not receive the decision are uncertain. They must query other cohorts or wait for the coordinator to recover to learn the outcome.

question: Compare and contrast the Bully election algorithm with the Ring election algorithm. Your answer should detail the steps of each algorithm and analyze their key differences in terms of message complexity, robustness, and the scenarios for which they are best suited.
expectedPoints: Bully Algorithm Steps: 1) A process notices the coordinator has failed and starts an election. 2) It sends ELECTION messages to all processes with higher IDs. 3) If no responses, it wins and announces itself as coordinator. 4) If it gets a response, that higher-ID process takes over the election.

Ring Algorithm Steps: 1) A process notices the coordinator has failed. 2) It creates an ELECTION message with its own ID and passes it to its neighbor. 3) Each process adds its own ID if it is higher than those in the message. 4) When the message returns to the initiator, the process with the highest ID becomes coordinator and circulates a COORDINATOR message.
Message Complexity: Bully algorithm has O(n²) worst-case message complexity. Ring algorithm has O(n) message complexity.
Robustness: The Bully algorithm is less robust to failures during the election process. The Ring algorithm is vulnerable to a single broken link breaking the logical ring.

Suitability: The Bully algorithm is best when message cost is less critical and processes have known, unique priorities (IDs). The Ring algorithm is better for systems with a logical ring topology where a lower message count is desirable.
